reasoning: The 3-month Treasury yield currently sits around 3.68%, tracking a federal funds rate of 3.50%-3.75%. Throughout 2026, most FOMC members and market participants anticipate rates to either hold steady or decline slightly (1-2 rate cuts are projected by many, though an oil shock from Iran has introduced some hawkish uncertainty). Over the short term (1 week to 1 month), rates will likely follow daily volatility, yielding a near-50% probability of an increase, tilted slightly downward due to the longer-term structural expectation of eventual easing. By 6 to 12 months out, the likelihood of rate cuts outweighs the likelihood of hikes, given median FOMC projections and explicit political pressure for lower rates (e.g., via the expected Fed transition to Kevin Warsh in May 2026). Thus, probabilities for 1 year drop to the ~30% range. For distant horizons (3 to 10 years), uncertainty increases significantly, reflecting unknown macroeconomic cycles, but given the neutral rate is generally estimated to be below 3.5%, the structural bias is still toward a value strictly lower than 3.68%, while keeping estimations progressively closer to 0.5 per instructions.
